differential_diagnoses: Penetrating duodenal ulcer causing retroperitoneal abscess and renal vein thrombosis
Lemierre-like syndrome involving Streptococcus anginosus with septic thrombophlebitis
Infective endocarditis with septic pulmonary emboli and secondary hypercoagulability
Strongyloides hyperinfection syndrome with systemic bacterial translocation
Systemic lupus erythematosus with secondary antiphospholipid syndrome and vasculitis
Malignancy (e.g., lymphoma or primary GI tumor) with paraneoplastic thrombosis and superinfection
Biliary sepsis with portal vein or inferior vena cava involvement
next_steps: Perform transesophageal echocardiography to rule out endocarditis
Perform esophagogastroduodenoscopy to evaluate duodenal pathology and rule out perforation
Consult interventional radiology for image-guided drainage of the pararenal collection
Re-evaluate anticoagulation strategy based on risk of bleeding from duodenal pathology
Obtain serial blood cultures to monitor for clearance of bacteremia
Obtain stool examination for ova and parasites to exclude Strongyloides

differential_diagnoses: Penetrating duodenal ulcer with secondary retroperitoneal abscess and septic thrombophlebitis
Strongyloides hyperinfection syndrome with bacterial translocation and systemic sepsis
Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome (CAPS) triggered by occult infection
Primary GI malignancy (e.g., adenocarcinoma or lymphoma) with superinfection and paraneoplastic thrombosis
Infective endocarditis with systemic septic emboli
next_steps: Perform image-guided aspiration/drainage of the retroperitoneal/pararenal collection for aerobic, anaerobic, and fungal cultures
Consult Gastroenterology for urgent esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) to evaluate the duodenal lesion
Send serum for Strongyloides IgG serology and evaluate for HTLV-1 co-infection
Continue antibiotic therapy tailored to S. anginosus while monitoring for endocarditis via echocardiography
Maintain therapeutic anticoagulation while carefully monitoring for gastrointestinal bleeding risk
Avoid empiric high-dose corticosteroids until strongyloidiasis is definitively ruled out
